title: Show Priority In CLI Bead Output
description: Update CLI display surfaces so high-priority beads are visible in `bead show` and `bead list --plain` without adding noise for normal-priority beads.
acceptance_criteria: `takt bead show <id>` includes `priority: high` only for high-priority beads.
`takt bead show <id>` omits any priority line for normal-priority beads.
`takt bead list --plain` includes a `PRIORITY` column showing `high` for high-priority beads and blank for normal beads.
Display updates preserve existing output shape aside from the new priority visibility requirements.

handoff_summary.completed: Both acceptance criteria implemented: (1) PRIORITY column added to LIST_PLAIN_COLUMNS in formatting.py with blank for None via new _column_value dispatcher; (2) bead show now strips priority from the output dict when None.
handoff_summary.risks: The existing test at test_cli_bead.py:309 iterates LIST_PLAIN_COLUMNS and asserts all headers appear in the output — adding PRIORITY column means it will now also assert PRIORITY is present, which is correct. The line at :313 checks `" - "` appears for feature_root_id/parent_id; priority renders as blank (not "-"), so that assertion is unaffected.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester should verify: (1) bead list --plain header row contains PRIORITY; (2) normal-priority bead rows show blank in PRIORITY column; (3) high-priority bead rows show "high" in PRIORITY column; (4) bead show JSON omits "priority" key for normal beads; (5) bead show JSON includes "priority": "high" for high-priority beads; (6) regression: existing plain output columns/alignment unchanged.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: The PRIORITY column is inserted between TYPE and TITLE in LIST_PLAIN_COLUMNS. Any test that checks column order positionally (e.g. by split index) may need updating. The existing test at :309 checks headers by substring presence, so it is safe — but verify no other test relies on column position.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Two-function dispatch pattern: `_column_value(attribute, value)` wraps `_plain_value` and adds special handling for `priority` (blank instead of "-" for None). This avoids changing the LIST_PLAIN_COLUMNS tuple type (no breaking change to callers that iterate the tuple) while enabling per-column formatting. Alternative considered: extend column tuple to 3-tuple with optional formatter callable — rejected as over-engineered for a single special case. For `bead show`, the dict is mutated in the command handler rather than in `Bead.to_dict()`, preserving `to_dict()` as a lossless serializer for internal use while making the CLI display omit null priority.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Test `bead list --plain` with: (a) no beads — "No beads found." still emitted; (b) one normal-priority bead — PRIORITY column present in header, blank in data row; (c) one high-priority bead — PRIORITY column shows "high"; (d) mix of both — correct values per row. Test `bead show` with: (a) normal-priority bead — "priority" key absent from JSON output; (b) high-priority bead — "priority": "high" present. Regression: column widths still align, existing column order preserved, no "-" bleed into PRIORITY column.

conflict_risks: The PRIORITY column is inserted between TYPE and TITLE in LIST_PLAIN_COLUMNS. Any test that checks column order positionally (e.g. by split index) may need updating. The existing test at :309 checks headers by substring presence, so it is safe — but verify no other test relies on column position.

execution_history.summary: Deferred due to file-scope conflict with active bead B-8927afc7

Added priority visibility to CLI bead output. `bead list --plain` now has a PRIORITY column (blank for normal, "high" for high-priority). `bead show` now omits the `priority` key entirely for normal-priority beads and includes `priority: "high"` for high-priority beads.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
